title: PDF attachments are corrupted after upload
body: When customers upload PDF files to their tasks, the files come through broken. You can't open them — the PDF viewer says the file is damaged. This is happening for multiple customers. We use the file attachment feature heavily so this is really impacting our workflow.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — corrupted attachments definitely sounds disruptive, especially if you're relying on them heavily.

To help us understand what's going on, could you walk me through exactly what you're doing when this happens? For example: where in TaskFlow are you when you attach the PDF, how are you uploading it (drag-and-drop, the attach button, etc.), and what happens right after — does the upload appear to succeed normally, or do you notice anything unusual at that point?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Look, we just upload PDFs the normal way — click the attach button on a task, pick the file, it uploads, says it's done. No errors, no warnings, nothing weird. Then when someone tries to open it later, it's garbage. I don't know what else to tell you about the upload process, it looks like it works fine. The file is just corrupted when you download it. Can you just look into it? This has been going on for about a week and our team is stuck.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Totally understand the frustration — let's get this figured out. You mentioned this started about a week ago, which means attachments were working fine before that. Can you think of anything that changed around that time — any updates to your workflow, different types of PDFs you're working with, or even a notice from your team about a TaskFlow update?

Also, when you say the files come through broken — is that *every* PDF, or have some made it through okay? If some work, anything you've noticed that's different about the ones that don't (file size, where they came from, how they were created)?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I don't know about any updates, I'm not the IT person here. All I know is it was working before and now it's not. As for which ones work — actually yeah, now that you mention it, I think some smaller PDFs might have been okay? Like quick one-pagers and stuff. But the bigger reports and contracts are definitely broken. I don't know why that would matter though. Can someone just fix this already?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That detail about smaller PDFs working is actually really helpful — it gives us a strong thread to pull on.

Two things that would help us zero in on this:

1. Have you (or anyone on your team) tried uploading other types of files recently — like Word docs, images, or spreadsheets? If so, are those coming through okay, or are they also broken when they're larger? That'll help us figure out if this is something about how we handle PDFs specifically or a broader upload issue.

2. If you have one of the broken PDFs handy, could you check something for me? Compare the file size of the original on your computer to the size of the downloaded (broken) version — even a rough comparison like "the original is 5 MB but the download is only 2 MB" would tell us a lot about what's going wrong under the hood.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I don't know, I haven't really paid attention to other file types. We mostly deal with PDFs. Maybe some images looked a little off but I figured that was just my screen or something. I'm not going to go compare file sizes — I have a team of 15 people waiting on me to get this resolved. The PDFs are broken, they were fine two weeks ago, and now they're not. That's what I know.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Large file attachments silently corrupted during upload/download (size-dependent truncation)","problem":"PDF attachments uploaded to tasks via the attach button appear to upload successfully (no errors), but are corrupted and unopenable when later downloaded. The issue is size-dependent: small one-page PDFs work fine, while larger multi-page reports and contracts are broken. The reporter also noted images may have looked 'a little off,' suggesting the problem is not PDF-specific. The issue began approximately 1-2 weeks ago; attachments worked correctly before that.","root_cause_hypothesis":"A recent change (likely a deployment, infrastructure update, or configuration change in the past 1-2 weeks) introduced a file size limit or broke chunked/multipart upload handling, causing large files to be silently truncated or corrupted during upload, storage, or download. The absence of any client-side error suggests the truncation happens server-side after the upload is accepted.","reproduction_steps":["Create or open a task in TaskFlow","Click the attach button and upload a small PDF (< 1 MB, single page) — confirm it downloads correctly","Upload a larger PDF (multi-page, several MB) using the same method","Download the larger PDF and attempt to open it — expect it to be reported as damaged/corrupted","Compare the file size of the uploaded original to the downloaded file to confirm truncation"],"environment":"Affects multiple users (team of 15). No specific browser, OS, or network details provided, but the issue is consistent across the team, suggesting a server-side problem rather than a client-specific one.","severity":"high","impact":"A team of 15 is blocked from using file attachments, which is central to their workflow. The issue has persisted for 1-2 weeks. Any customer relying on attachments for files above the threshold is likely affected.","recommended_fix":"1. Check deployment/change history for the past 2 weeks for anything touching file upload, storage, or download paths (e.g., multipart upload config, proxy body-size limits, storage gateway changes). 2. Compare stored file sizes in the backend to known originals to confirm truncation vs. encoding corruption. 3. Check for recently introduced file size limits (e.g., nginx client_max_body_size, API gateway payload limits, cloud storage chunking config). 4. If chunked uploads are used, verify chunk reassembly logic. 5. Test with various file sizes to identify the exact threshold where corruption begins.","proposed_test_case":"Upload files at various sizes (100 KB, 1 MB, 5 MB, 10 MB, 25 MB) across multiple file types (PDF, PNG, DOCX). Download each and verify byte-for-byte integrity against the original using checksums. The test should assert that downloaded file hash matches uploaded file hash for all sizes up to the documented maximum.","information_gaps":["Exact file size threshold where corruption begins (determinable from server-side testing)","Whether the corruption is truncation vs. encoding damage (determinable by comparing stored file sizes)","Which specific deployment or config change caused the regression (determinable from change history)","Whether the issue affects all file types equally or varies (partially answered — PDFs confirmed, images possibly affected)"]}
